segments.s341-g9: Word order is compressed: 'بدیشان بدی جان لهراسپ شاد' literally 'to-them misfortune soul Lohrasp happy'. The topical/dative phrase 'بدیشان' ('to/for them')+noun 'بدی' ('misfortune/harm') sets the cause; 'جان لهراسپ' ('the soul/life of Lohrasp') is the subject whose state follows, and 'شاد' is a predicate adjective ('was glad'). The copula is omitted (past state is implied by surrounding narration).
segments.s341-g10: 'وزیشان' is a compounded/poetic particle here meaning roughly 'and because of them' (the lexical entry given doesn't match exactly; treat as a connective referring back to 'بدیشان'). 'نکردی' is the past negative of 'کردن' ('did not do'); here it functions with the noun 'یاد' to mean 'did not remember'. 'ز' is the preposition linking to the object 'گشتاسپ' ('of/from Gushtasp'), and 'یاد' is the noun 'remembrance' used as the object of 'نکردی' (i.e. 'did not make remembrance of Gushtasp').
segments.s341-g11: 'که' introduces an explanatory subordinate clause ('for/that'). 'گشتاسپ را' uses the enclitic 'را' to mark/disambiguate the topic/object (here topicalizing Gushtasp). The phrase 'سر پر از باد بود' literally 'head full of wind was' — past copula 'بود' marks the state; 'باد' literally 'wind' is idiomatic for conceit or empty-headedness.
segments.s341-g12: 'وزان' ('and/from that') refers back to the previous clause ('because of that'); it links causally to the following statement. 'کار' ('affair/deed') is the thing causing the effect; 'لهراسپ ناشاد بود' is a predication: 'Lohrasp was unhappy' with 'ناشاد' as predicate adjective and 'بود' the past copula.
segments.s341-g13: 'چنین' ('thus/so') sets the manner. 'تا' here is temporal 'until', introducing the temporal clause 'تا برآمد برین روزگار'—'until this time arose/arrived'. 'برآمد' is past 'arose/appeared'; 'برین' is the older/poetic form of 'این' ('this') attached after the preposition 'بر'; 'روزگار' is the noun 'time/age/period'.
segments.s341-g14: 'پر از درد' is an idiom 'full of pain/sorrow' with the genitive/linking preposition 'از'. The phrase 'گشتاسپ از شهریار' indicates who experiences the pain and its source: 'Gushtasp (was) [in] pain because of the king'—the second 'از' marks cause ('because of'). The clause as a whole reports the resulting suffering.
segments.s341-g15: 'چنان بد که' is a correlative 'so/bad that...' introducing a result clause. 'در پارس' is a locative prepositional phrase 'in Pars (Persia)'. 'یک روز' is temporal 'one day', and 'تخت' ('throne') is the direct object/topic: the thing that follows in the result clause (what was done).
segments.s341-g16: 'نهادند' is the past plural form 'they placed/they set' (the action performed). 'زیر' is the postposition 'under/beneath' governing the following noun phrase 'گل‌افشان درخت' — 'گل‌افشان' is a compound adjective 'flower‑scattering / flower‑strewing' modifying 'درخت' ('tree'). The agent 'they' remains implicit (typical epic narration).
segments.s341-g73: ببینید is an imperative/imperative-like vocative “see/look” (presented here as a narrative address). گفت is the past verb “said” introducing reported speech; این‌که is the conjunction ‘that’ (introducing the content of what was said). گشتاسپ کرد is a simple past clause “Gushtasp did” (کرد is the past of کردن ‘to do’). Word order: verb-final Persian clauses place the past verb کرد at the end to complete the report.
segments.s341-g74: دلم کرد is an idiomatic past construction: دل + ـم (دلم “my heart”) followed by کرد ‘made/was made’ functioning here like “became / was filled (for me).” پر before a noun means “full (of) …” and takes the following noun as the content. Thus دلم کرد پرِ درد = “my heart became full of pain.” The second half parallels the first: سر پر ز گرد = “head full from/of dust,” where ز marks source/contents (“with/of”).
segments.s341-g75: بپروردمش is past tense “I raised/nurtured him”: ب‑ prefix + پروردم (I nurtured) + ـش object suffix (him). تا here expresses purpose/result (“so that / in order that”), linking the raising to the following result. برآورد یال is a verb + object phrase: برآورد (brought forth/caused to appear) with یال “mane/crest” as its object—literary word order places the verb before the object-predicate in this short clause.
segments.s341-g76: شد is the past “became.” اندر is an archaic/preclassical preposition meaning “in/within.” جهان نامور is the predicate “renowned in the world.” بی‌همال is an epithet attached to نامور meaning “without equal / peerless” (poetic adjective). Overall: “he became renowned in the world, without equal.”
segments.s341-g77: بدانگه “thereupon / at that moment” is a temporal/discourse connector. که introduces a subordinate clause; گفتم is “I said” (here reported by the narrator). The second که begins the clause reported by گفتم. آمد is past “came” or “came into being”; به بار (an idiom: به بار آمدن) means “to come to fruition / to mature / to bear fruit” — تا/به link shows result. Word order: the connective and the repeated که frame the reported action ‘he came to bearing.’
segments.s341-g78: ز باغ من = “from my garden” (ز = from). آواره شد = “became wandering / became an exile” (آواره here adjectival/predicative). نامدار is a predicate adjective “famed/renowned.” The clause states that as a result he left the speaker’s garden and became a famed wanderer.
segments.s341-g79: برفت is a past-tense verb ‘he departed / he went away’ (note: an older form of رفت). The coordination و links the next idea: بر اندیشه بر بود دیر is a compressed poetic order meaning “he was long upon thought” — بر (upon) + اندیشه (thought) + بود (was) + دیر (long). The doubled preposition بر + بر is a poetic repetition emphasizing the locative ‘upon thought.’
segments.s341-g80: بفرمود is an honorific/polite past verb “he commanded/ordered/said.” تا introduces the purpose/result clause: تا پیش او شد زریر = “so that / until Zarrir came before him.” پیش او شد is “came into his presence” (پیش = before/in the presence of; شد = became/came). زریر is the proper name of the person summoned.
segments.s341-g17: بفرمود is a polite/past reporting verb meaning 'he ordered/said' (honorific past); it is followed directly by the proper name لهراسپ as the addressee/agent of the command. تا introduces a subordinate clause with the purposive/command sense 'so that / that', and مهتران is the subject of that subordinate clause (classical plural for 'leaders/elders/commanders').
segments.s341-g18: برفتند is a past plural verb 'they went/they departed' (classical/poetic form with the prefix بر-). چندی ز لشکر سران is a partitive phrase: چندی 'several/some', ز (poetic genitive marker 'from'), لشکر 'army', سران 'the leaders/heads' — together 'several of the army's chiefs'. Word order places the partitive before the head noun, and the verb agrees with the plural 'they' (the chiefs).
segments.s341-g19: به خوان بر is an adverbial phrase 'to/at the table/banquet' (به = toward/to; خوان = banquet; بر here = 'toward/onto' in a locative/directional sense). یکی جام is the direct object 'one cup' (numeral + noun). می‌خواستند is the past/plural imperfective 'they wished/asked'—here used to express that attendants desired a cup to be brought.
segments.s341-g20: دل شاه گیتی is a genitive chain 'the heart of the king of the world' (دل = heart; شاه = king; گیتی = world). بیاراستند is a past plural verb 'they adorned/they beautified' (بی- prefix + آراستن), so the whole clause means 'they adorned the king of the world's heart' (i.e., they delighted the king).
segments.s341-g21: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'. گشتاسپ is the subject name. می‌خورد is the historical/imperfective form 'was eating' or simply 'ate' in narrative style. برپای خاست is a light-verb phrase 'rose to his feet / stood up' (برپای = 'on his feet/standing', خاست = past 'arose'), so the clause sets a temporal sequence: while/when he was eating, he stood up.
segments.s341-g22: چنین گفت is a formula 'thus he said' (چنین = thus; گفت = 'said' — here used as the reporting verb). کای شاه is a vocative 'O king' (کای = O), and با داد و راست is an adverbial/qualifying phrase 'with justice and right/truth' (با = with; داد = justice; راست = right/truth). The clause is the beginning of his direct address.
segments.s341-g23: به شاهی نشست is an idiomatic verbal phrase 'to sit in kingship / to take the throne' (به = into/in; شاهی = kingship/title; نشست = 'sat/settled'). تو is the addressed 'you'. فرخنده باد is an optative blessing 'may it be auspicious' where باد is the optative particle 'may'. The whole segment is a benediction wishing a fortunate reign.
segments.s341-g24: همان جاودان نام تو is a noun phrase 'that (very) eternal name of yours' (همان/that; جاودان = eternal; نام تو = your name). زنده‌باد is another optative formula 'may (it) live / long live (it)'. Together this is a wish that his (perpetual) name endure forever.
segments.s341-g49: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'. گشتاسب is the subject (a proper name). بشنید is the simple past 'he heard' (here with the verbal prefix b- common in Classical Persian). شد is the copular/predicative 'became', and پر ز درد is a predicative phrase meaning 'full of pain' (پر 'full (of)' + ز 'of/from' + درد 'pain/sorrow'), so the clause reads 'When Gushasp heard (it), he became full of pain.'
segments.s341-g50: بیامد is the past 'he came'. The prepositional bundle ز پیش means 'from (or out of) before / from the presence of' and links to پدر 'father' as the reference; گونه زرد is a verbless descriptive phrase 'cheek pale' (گونه 'cheek', زرد 'pale/wan') describing his appearance as he came before his father.
segments.s341-g51: همی + past verb expresses a repeated or ongoing past action ('he kept saying'). گفت here functions as the verb 'said/spoke'. بیگانگان را uses را as the definite object marker ('the strangers' as object). نواز is an imperative/command-stem meaning 'treat kindly, entertain, show favour to' directed at the addressee.
segments.s341-g52: چنین باش is an imperative: چنین 'thus/so' + باش 'be!' → 'be thus' or 'act so'. و is the coordinating conjunction 'and'. با + زاده forms a prepositional phrase 'with (your) offspring' (زاده 'offspring/child'). هرگز مساز is a negative imperative: هرگز 'never' + مساز 'do not make/do not join' — together 'never consort/associate (with them)'.
segments.s341-g53: ز لشکر 'from the army/host' (poetic preposition z). ورا (to him / for him) indicates possession/assignment in older usage; بود is the past copula 'there was/were'. سیصد سوار is the noun phrase 'three hundred horsemen' functioning as the subject/experiencer of بود ('there were three hundred horsemen').
segments.s341-g54: همه 'all' is the subject reference to those horsemen. گرد here is a short poetic adjective/adverb used in description (in context it helps qualify the troops); و is 'and', شایسته 'worthy/fit', کارزار 'for battle/war' — together 'all (of them) ... fit for battle'. This is a compact adjectival description applied to the previously mentioned horsemen.
segments.s341-g55: فرود آمد is a compound verbal phrase 'he descended / alighted' (فرود 'descent' + آمد 'came'). و is the conjunction 'and'. کهتران را with را marks the object 'the quehterān' (an older/poetic group-name here 'the lesser/younger/other men') and بخواند is used in the sense 'he called/summoned' (بخواند here = to call together).
segments.s341-g56: همه رازها 'all the secrets' (همه 'all' + رازها plural 'secrets') is the direct object. پیش ایشان is a locative/prepositional phrase 'before them / in their presence' (پیش = 'in the presence of', ایشان = 'them'). براند is a verb here meaning 'set forth / put before / present' in a poetic sense (built on the root راند but used transitively), so the clause says he presented/all revealed the secrets before them.
segments.s341-g41: چو is the subordinating conjunction 'when' introducing a temporal clause. اندرز is the noun 'counsel/advice' and KiKhosrow (کیخسرو) is the name in genitive relation: 'the counsel of Kay Khosrow'. آرم here is an archaic/poetic finite verb form meaning 'I put/bring (to mind)' or 'I recall' (the dataset's glosses list forms of 'rest', but in this context it functions as the main verb of the subordinate clause). به یاد is a prepositional phrase 'to/into memory' — lit. 'into remembrance' — completing the verb: 'when I put (it) into memory.'
segments.s341-g42: تو is the addressee 'you'. بشنو is an imperative/jussive 'listen' / 'heed'. نگر is a short imperative 'look!' or 'consider!'; it is exhortative and frames the warning. سر نپیچی is a negative command: ن- (negative) + پیچی (2nd sg. present/subjunctive/imperative of پیچیدن 'to turn/twist') — 'do not turn (your) head'. ز داد is a prepositional phrase 'from/of justice' with ز (from/because of) + داد 'justice' — 'away from justice' or 'from justice'.
segments.s341-g43: مرا is the clitic object 'me' (here in dative/accusative role) and precedes گفت 'said' in the common poetic order 'said to me'. بیدادگر شهریار is a noun + epithet phrase 'the unjust/tyrannical king' (epithet before or after the head noun in poetry is flexible). The whole segment therefore means 'the unjust king said to me.'
segments.s341-g44: یکی is an indefinite 'one' / 'a certain'. خو here is the noun 'sleep' or 'dream' (contextually likely 'a sleep/dream' or figurative rest). بود is the past copula 'was'. پیش is a locative/prepositional adverb 'before/in front of' (here 'before' in space or time). باغ بهار is a compound 'the spring garden' (باغ + بهار). Word order is subject (one sleep) + past verb + locative phrase.
segments.s341-g45: که چون begins a subordinate clause ('for/that when'); here چون functions temporally 'when'. آب is the subject 'water'. یابد is a present/subjunctive/indicative form of یافتن 'to obtain/attain' meaning 'attains/gains'. به نیرو is a prepositional phrase 'to/with force' or 'in power' and شود 'becomes' is the present/future copula—together: 'when the water gains strength, it becomes (powerful)'.
segments.s341-g46: همه باغ is 'the whole garden' (همه modifies باغ). ازو is a fused prepositional pronoun 'from it / because of it' (referring back to the water). پر ز آهو شود is a passive/result clause: پر 'full (of)' + ز 'of/from' + آهو 'gazelle' + شود 'becomes' — 'becomes full of gazelles.' Word order places the result after the cause ('because of it the whole garden becomes full of gazelles').
segments.s341-g47: جوانی is a direct address 'O youth/young man' (vocative). هنوز 'still/yet' negates the urgency. این بلندی 'this loftiness/high station' is the object (بلندی = height/loftiness here); مجوی is a negative imperative/jussive 'do not seek' (مَج + جوی form: poetic negative command). The segment cautions the youth not to seek elevated rank yet.
segments.s341-g48: سخن را is the object phrase 'the speech/words' with را marking definiteness/emphasis. بسنج is the imperative 'weigh/measure/consider' (command to judge one's words). و 'and' links clauses. به اندازه گوی is an exhortation 'speak to (the) measure' — به اندازه 'with moderation / in due measure' + گوی (poetic imperative/optative of گفتن) 'speak/tell'. Together: 'weigh your words and speak in moderation.'
segments.s341-g1: دو فرزند بودش is an introductory nominal phrase meaning 'there were to him two children / he had two sons' — بودش is an older/enclitic form of 'بود' (to be) implying possession or existence. به کردار ماه is a prepositional phrase modifying those sons: به + کردار + ماه = 'in manner/appearance like the moon' (a poetic simile for beauty). Word order is subject phrase → predicate/existential verb → modifying prepositional phrase.
segments.s341-g2: سزاوار governs the following nouns and means 'worthy/deserving of'; here it predicates of the two sons. شاهی، تخت و کلاه are a paratactic list (no ezāfe linking) of things they deserve: 'kingship, throne, and crown' — کلاه in epic usage stands for the royal headgear (crown). The conjunction و simply links items.
segments.s341-g3: یکی نام گشتاسپ و دیگر زریر is a simple enumeration: 'one (of them) named Gushtasp and the other Zarir.' نام precedes the proper name in this formulaic construction to mark 'named X.' دیگری here means 'the other (one)'.
segments.s341-g4: که introduces a relative clause modifying زریر: 'who ...'. زیر آوریدی is an archaic past form (آوریدی ~ آَوردی) with the preverb/particle زیر giving the sense 'to bring under / to humble / to lay low.' The verb is second/third‑person past in older epic style and the object سر ('head') precedes the verb (typical SOV order). نره شیر = 'male lion.'
segments.s341-g5: گذشته here functions as a predicative participle/verb meaning 'he passed beyond / excelled.' به هر دانشی از پدر is the comparative phrase 'in every kind of knowledge than (or compared to) his father' — از پدر supplies the standard of comparison. The construction is compact and typical of epic diction.
segments.s341-g6: ز لشکر 'from the army/host' (ز poetic = از). به مردی برآورده سر is best read as 'he raised up / promoted a man to be (برآورده) a head/chief' — برآورده is a past participle here with the sense 'elevated/appointed,' and سر is used figuratively as 'chief/commander.' Word order places the source (ز لشکر) first, then the beneficiary (به مردی) and the resultant rank (برآورده سر).
segments.s341-g7: دو شاه سرافراز و دو نیک‌پی is a symmetric noun phrase enumerating titles/qualities: 'two exalted/triumphant kings and two of noble bearing.' نیک‌پی (compound) signals good lineage or virtue. The adjective follows the noun as is common in Persian epic diction.
segments.s341-g8: نبیره جهاندار کاوس کی names the lineage: 'offspring/scions of the world-holder (جهاندار) Kaūs (کاوس) Ki.' In epic usage کاوس کی / کی کاوس is the royal name 'Kay Kavus' (کی here functions as the archaic royal title 'Kay' attached to the name). The phrase as a whole says these are descendants of that sovereign; syntactically نبیره is the head and the following two words identify whose scions they are.
segments.s341-g65: This is a conditional clause introduced by the archaic compound conjunction که‌گر (if/if even). زی here functions as a comparative preposition 'than/from' and combines with من (I) and the following verb phrase to express 'than I' (i.e. 'lower than I'). آیی is a present/subjunctive-form verb 'you come/you are' (here stative: 'you are'), and ت + را together mark the second-person object/topic (colloquial/poetic clitic order is broken out as two items in the segmentation). کهترم is a predicative adjective 'I/am lesser' (literally 'lower'), so the clause literally reads 'if (that) from me you are lower' → 'if you are lower than I.'
segments.s341-g66: ز is the poetic preposition 'from' (here introducing the domain one will not abandon). فرمان و رای تو is a coordinated object: فرمان 'command, order' and رای 'counsel, advice/plan', modified by the pronominal تو 'your'. برنگذرم is a negative present/future-type verb form meaning 'I will not turn aside/pass over' (i.e. 'I will not abandon/disobey'); it is the main verb of the conditional clause, whose subject is implicit first person.
segments.s341-g67: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'; شب تیره شد is a simple past clause 'the night became dark' (شد = became). با سپه is an instrumental/associative phrase 'with the host/army', and برنشست (past) means 'he rose/took his seat' or 'took his place' (here: took his station with the army). Subject is omitted (third person).
segments.s341-g68: همی is an archaic continuous/aspectual particle marking ongoing action (like 'kept on' or 'was'), so همی رفت = 'he went on/advanced'. جوشان is an adverbial/participial adjective 'seething/boiling/raging' modifying the motion. و simply links the second phrase, and گرزی به دست is a prepositional phrase 'with a mace in (his) hand' (به ... دست = 'in the hand of'); word order places the instrument after the verb phrase.
segments.s341-g69: به here marks the agent/means in a passive-like construction ('by' the night-watch); شبگیر is the nocturnal watcher/night-guard (agent), لهراسپ is the proper name Lohrasp (the patient), and آگاه شد is a two-word predicate 'became aware / was alerted' (شد = became). Together: 'Lohrasp was made aware by the night-watch.'
segments.s341-g70: This is two coordinated clauses: غمی گشت = 'sorrow arose/there came grief' (گشت = became/turned into/arose). و links the clause to شادیش کوتاه شد = 'his joy became short' (شادیش = 'his happiness/joy', کوتاه شد = 'was shortened/was short-lived'). The subject of both verbs is impersonal or refers back to Lohrasp.
segments.s341-g71: ز لشکر is a poetic 'from the army' (ز = 'from'), جهاندیدگان is the object meaning 'those experienced in the world' (here: seasoned veterans/old campaigners), را is the definite object marker tagging جهاندیدگان, and بخواند (past) is the verb 'he summoned/called together' (from خواندن used in the sense 'to call/summon').
segments.s341-g72: همه بودنی is a phrase meaning 'all those who were (present)' or 'everyone existing/there'; پیش ایشان is a prepositional phrase 'before them / in their presence' (پیش = in front of/in the presence of; ایشان = them), and براند (past) is 'he drove/put/marshaled' (transitive 'to drive/bring forward'). Together: 'he marshaled everyone present before them.'
segments.s341-g81: بدو گفت = 'he said to him' (بدو = to him; گفت is past 'said' used here; the speaker addresses someone). بگزین is an imperative 'choose' that takes its object after it; ز لشکر 'from the army' is a prepositional phrase giving the source of what is to be chosen. Word order is typical Persian: indirect object phrase + speech-verb + imperative + source phrase.
segments.s341-g82: سواران is the noun 'horsemen/riders' (the force to be deployed). گرد here functions adverbially 'around/about' (indicating encirclement). از در کارزار is a prepositional phrase 'from/out of the gate of the battlefield' with در = gate and کارزار = battlefield; together it means 'around the battlefield gate.'
segments.s341-g83: برو is the imperative 'go' and تیز is an adverbial qualifier 'swiftly/quickly' placed after the verb (poetic order). بر سوی هندوستان is a compound directional phrase 'toward India' (بر سوی = toward; هندوستان = India).
segments.s341-g84: مبادا is a negative jussive/admonition 'may it not be' or 'lest / do not' introducing a warning. بر here means 'upon/on' (i.e., to or upon the land), و = 'and', بوم = 'homeland/dwelling', and جادوستان is the compound/attributive 'of the sorcerers' or 'land of magic' — together 'the land and dwellings of the sorcerers.' The clause is elliptical: the speaker warns against something happening to that land or against going onto it.
segments.s341-g85: سوی روم is a directional phrase 'toward Rome.' گستهم and نوذر here are proper names (Gostaham and Nowzar) functioning as the subject(s). برفت is the past-tense verb 'went' (the action applying to the named persons). The order places the direction first, then the persons, then the verb — a common poetic order.
segments.s341-g86: سوی چین is the directional phrase 'toward China.' The following sequence (گرازه گرازید تفت) appears as a list of names (three proper nouns) to which the motion verb of the previous clause is understood to apply; Persian poetry often omits repeating the verb. Grammatically, these items are in apposition as additional subjects directed toward China.
segments.s341-g33: چو is a temporal conjunction 'when' introducing the clause. Word order is Conjunction + Subject (کیخسرو) + prepositional phrase (از تو 'concerning you') + predicate complement (پر اندیشه 'full of thought') + verb (گشت, past of گشتن 'became/turned'). پر is an adjectival complement meaning 'full (of)'.
segments.s341-g34: ترا is the older/poetic order of the object pronoun + object marker (ت + را) meaning 'to you' (the subject Kay Khosrow is omitted and carried over from the previous clause). داد is the past verb 'gave' and تخت is its object 'throne'. In the second half خود اندر گذشت, خود is emphatic 'he himself' and اندر گذشت (گذشت in past) means 'passed on/withdrew' (an intransitive past action).
segments.s341-g35: گر is the conditional 'if'. ایدونک is a temporal/confirmatory particle meaning roughly 'now/at this time'. هستم is first-person present 'I am'. ز + ارزانیان expresses membership 'of/from the bestowers' = 'among the generous'. The clause is protasis (the 'if' part).
segments.s341-g36: This clause is elliptic (no overt verb): مرا نام is a poetic inversion for 'my name' with مرا as the accusative/possessive element placed before نام. را is the object marker. بر is the preposition 'on/upon', followed by a list of targets (تاج, تخت, کیان). The understood verb (e.g. 'place' or 'let it be placed') is omitted, typical in poetry.
segments.s341-g37: چنین هم 'thus also' sets the tone. که‌ام is the fused relative/particle + copula 'that I am' (که + ام). پیش تو is the locative/presence phrase 'before you/in your presence'. بنده‌وار is an adverbial/qualifying word 'servant-like, humbly', describing the manner.
segments.s341-g38: همی is an old/high register particle indicating continued or durative aspect; باشم is first-person subjunctive/optative 'may I be/remain' (so the clause expresses a wish). خوانمت is 'I call/address you' where -ت is the object suffix 'you', and شهریار is the complement 'king/sovereign' (i.e. 'I call you sovereign').
segments.s341-g39: به گشتاسپ گفت is 'he said to Gushtasp' (به marks the indirect object; گفت is the past verb 'said'). ای پسر is a vocative 'O son'. گوش دار is an imperative idiom 'pay attention/listen' (گوش 'ear/attention' + دار, an imperative particle meaning 'have/hold' in this collocation).
segments.s341-g40: که introduces the content clause ('that/for'); تندی is 'haste/rashness'. نه is the negator placed before the predicate 'خوب آید' ('does not suit/come well'), where آید (present subjunctive/indicative) literally 'comes' but idiomatically 'becomes/fits'. از شهریار 'from a king' indicates the source/agent for whom rashness is unsuitable.
segments.s341-g57: که here is the conjunction introducing reported speech ('that' / 'so'); امشب is the temporal adverb 'tonight'; همه is the subject 'everyone'. ساز رفتن کنید is an imperative-periphrasis: ساز (literally 'tune/arrangement') combines with the infinitive رفتن 'to go' and the 2nd-person-plural imperative کنید 'do/make' to mean 'prepare to leave' or 'make ready to go'.
segments.s341-g58: دل و دیده is a coordinated object phrase 'heart and eye' (دل = heart, دیده = eye). زین is an archaic/poetic contraction of 'از این' meaning 'from this' (here 'from this court')—this sense does not match the provided senses, so it is marked -1. بارگه is the locative 'royal court'; برکنید is the 2nd-person-plural imperative 'remove/dismiss' (here 'turn away' or 'take away').
segments.s341-g59: یکی functions as 'one' / 'someone (one of them)'. گفت here is the past verb 'said' (the provided sense list lacked 'said', so it is marked -1). از ایشان means 'from them' / 'among them' (ازیشان). که introduces the embedded question/content clause. راهت is راه + enclitic -ت 'your way/route'; کجاست is the copular question 'where is'.
segments.s341-g60: چو is a temporal/conditional conjunction 'when/if'. برداری is 2nd-person singular present/subjunctive of برداشتن here meaning 'you take/you remove' (in context 'when you depart'); آرامگاهت is 'your tomb/resting-place' (آرامگاه + -ت). کجاست is again the copular question 'where is'. Word order is typical: temporal clause (چو برداری) followed by the questioned predicate (آرامگاهت کجاست).
segments.s341-g61: چنین داد پاسخ is a short clause 'gave such an answer' — چنین 'thus/so', داد past tense of دادن 'gave', and پاسخ 'answer' as the object. که begins the content clause that follows; در هندوان is the locative 'among the Indians/Hindus'.
segments.s341-g62: مرا شاد دارند means literally 'they have/make me happy': مرا is the object pronoun 'me' (here split in the data as 'م' + 'را'); دارند is the present plural 'they have/hold' used idiomatically as 'they make/keep [me]'. و connects the next epithet; روشن‌روان is an epithet 'bright‑souled' or 'cheerful' qualifying the effect. (The token 'م' in the provided senses did not match a pronoun sense, so it is marked -1.)
segments.s341-g63: یکی functions as 'a certain one' or 'someone'. نامه دارم من is 'I have a letter' with دارم (I have) and the emphatic/subject pronoun من placed after the verb for emphasis. از شاه هند is the source phrase 'from the King of India'.
segments.s341-g64: نوشته here is the participial/ adjectival 'written' qualifying the letter. ز is the poetic/prepositional 'from/with' (instrumental sense: 'with'); مشک سیه = 'black musk' (the ink or perfume used for writing); بر پرند = 'upon a bird' or 'on a wing' (بر = on/upon, پرند = winged one/bird, i.e. a carrier bird). The whole phrase describes how the letter was written/transmitted.
segments.s341-g25: ترا is the clitic object 'to you' (ت second‑person suffix + را object marker) placed before the verb for emphasis. داد is the past tense 'gave'. یزدان is the subject 'God' (here after the verb, a flexible poetic word order). کلاه و کمر are the direct objects 'crown/hat and girdle/belt' that God gave.
segments.s341-g26: دگر functions as 'another' or 'besides' and introduces an additional giver. شاه کیخسرو is the noun phrase 'King Kay Khosrow.' دادگر is an epithet 'just' modifying the king. The clause is elliptical: 'another (giver) — the just King Kay Khosrow.'
segments.s341-g27: کنون is the adverb 'now.' من adds emphatic 'I.' یکی here is an indefinite/ humble 'one/a certain' modifying the following predicate. بنده‌ام is the predicate 'I am a servant' (بنده + enclitic -ام 'I am'). بر (here 'on/at') with درت ('your threshold/pearl') marks the locative 'at your court/door' (poetic use).
segments.s341-g28: پرستنده is a present‑participle/adjectival 'worshipping, devoted' describing the speaker. اختر و افسرت are the objects of that devotion — 'your star and your helmet/crown' — joined by و. The phrase qualifies the servant: 'a worshipper of your star and your helm.'
segments.s341-g29: ندارم is the negative present 'I do not have' (ن + دارم). کسی را is the (indefinite) object 'anyone/no one' with را as object marker; in the negative context it yields 'no one.' ز مردان is the poetic prepositional 'from/among the men.' به مرد is a comparative phrase 'equal to a man/hero' (به used in comparisons).
segments.s341-g30: گر is the conditional 'if.' آیند is third‑person plural present/subjunctive 'they come' (classical/poetic form used in conditions). پیشم 'before me/in my presence' follows the verb. به‌روز 'on the day' (literally 'to‑day' in the sense 'on the day of...') and نبرد 'battle' form the adverbial 'on the day of battle.'
segments.s341-g31: مگر here means 'except.' The sequence رستم زال سام سوار is a genealogical/name string: 'Rostam [son of] Zal [son of] Sam the horseman' (appositive chain identifying one person by lineage). سوار is an epithet 'horseman/rider' attached to Sam.
segments.s341-g32: که introduces the relative clause 'that/for whom.' با او 'with him' indicates opposition/contest. نسازد is negative + present form of ساختن used idiomatically: here 'to match/stand against' (ن + سازد ≈ 'no one matches him'). کسی in the negative context means 'no one.' کارزار is 'battle/contest.' Altogether: '...for whom no one can contend in battle.'

sentences.s341-s5: Lohrasp rejoiced at their misfortune, and he did not remember Gushtasp on their account.
sentences.s341-s6: For Gushtasp's head was full of wind (i.e. he was proud), and because of that affair Lohrasp was unhappy.
sentences.s341-s7: Thus, until that time rose, a period full of suffering came upon Gushtasp because of the king.
sentences.s341-s8: So dire was it that, in Pars, one day they set the throne beneath a flower‑scattering tree.
sentences.s341-s37: “See,” he said, “what Gushtasp did: my heart was filled with pain and my head filled with dust.”
sentences.s341-s38: I reared him so that his mane might come forth; he became famed in the world, unmatched.
sentences.s341-s39: Then — as I said — he came to ripeness; from my garden he became a wandering, famed one.
sentences.s341-s40: He departed and was long in thought; he commanded, and Zarrir came before him.
sentences.s341-s9: He ordered Lohrasp that the commanders should go forward; several of the army's chiefs set out.
sentences.s341-s10: At the banquet they asked for a single cup to be brought; they adorned the heart of the world's king.
sentences.s341-s11: When Gushtasp had been eating and rose to his feet, he said: 'O king, with justice and right...'
sentences.s341-s12: May you sit in kingship — may it be auspicious for you; may that eternal name of yours live.
sentences.s341-s25: When Gushasp heard [it], he was filled with grief; he came before his father, his cheek pale.
sentences.s341-s26: He kept saying, 'Treat strangers kindly; be thus, and never consort with (your) offspring.'
sentences.s341-s27: From his host there were three hundred horsemen; all were fit and proper for battle.
sentences.s341-s28: He descended and summoned the lesser men; he laid all the secrets before them.
sentences.s341-s21: When I bring Kay Khosrow's counsel to mind, you listen — look! do not turn your head away from justice.
sentences.s341-s22: The unjust king said to me: 'Once there was a sleep/dream before the spring-garden.'
sentences.s341-s23: For when the water attains force, it becomes powerful, and the whole garden because of it is filled with gazelles.
sentences.s341-s24: Young man, do not yet seek this high rank; weigh your speech and speak in measure.
sentences.s341-s1: He had two sons, with faces like the moon, worthy of kingship, the throne, and the crown.
sentences.s341-s2: One was named Gushtasp and the other Zarir, who had laid low the head of a male lion.
sentences.s341-s3: He excelled his father in every science; from the host he promoted (raised up) one man to be a chief.
sentences.s341-s4: Two proud kings and two noble-born scions of the world-ruler Kay Kavus.
sentences.s341-s33: If you are lower than I, I will not depart from your command and counsel.
sentences.s341-s34: When the night grew dark he took his place with the host; he went forth seething, a mace in his hand.
sentences.s341-s35: Lohrasp was alerted by the night-watch; sorrow came and his joy was shortened.
sentences.s341-s36: He summoned the seasoned veterans from the army and marshaled everyone present before them.
sentences.s341-s41: He said to him, "Choose a thousand from the army; have the horsemen circle about the gate of the battlefield."
sentences.s341-s42: Go swiftly toward India; beware (do not let anything happen to) the land and dwellings of the sorcerers.
sentences.s341-s43: Gostaham and Nowzar went toward Rome; (likewise) Garazeh, Gorazid, and Taft toward China.
sentences.s341-s17: When Kay Khosrow became full of thought about you, he gave you the throne and himself withdrew.
sentences.s341-s18: If I am now among the generous, place my name upon the crown, the throne, and among the Kian (the royal house).
sentences.s341-s19: Thus—just as I stand before you in a humble, servant-like way—may I remain so, and may I call you sovereign.
sentences.s341-s20: He said to Gushtasp, 'O son, listen: rashness does not become a good/fit king.'
sentences.s341-s29: That tonight everyone prepare to depart; remove heart and eye from this court.
sentences.s341-s30: One of them said, 'Where is your way? When you depart, where will your tomb be?'
sentences.s341-s31: He answered thus: 'Among the Indians they make me happy and bright‑souled.'
sentences.s341-s32: One said, 'I have a letter from the King of India, written in black musk on a bird.'
sentences.s341-s13: God gave you a crown and a girdle; another giver was King Kay Khosrow, the just.
sentences.s341-s14: Now I am but a single servant at your threshold, a worshipper of your star and your helmet.
sentences.s341-s15: I have no one among men equal to a hero; if they come before me on the day of battle...
sentences.s341-s16: Except Rostam — Rostam of Zal, (son of) Sam the horseman — with him no one can match in battle.
